slides.script: Lesson 1.3, Day 4. This is the last day of this lesson. The quiz is fifteen minutes, five questions at AP pacing. Full debrief after — we're naming the misconception each wrong answer was built to exploit. Then a bridge into Lesson 1.4.

3 attacks, 3 defenses, 2 adversary skill levels. 8 items. Fill them all in from memory before we start the quiz. This is deliberately hard — the retrieval attempt is the review. If you blank on one, that's the thing to check in your notes in the next 60 seconds.

Three strategies on the board for the entire quiz. Predict first before you read choices. Highlight the keywords — two of today's five questions turn on a single word. Slash the trash down to two choices before you commit. Go when I hand out the papers.
Fifteen minutes. No notes, no devices. Three minutes per question is real AP exam pacing — use the time. When the timer ends, pencils down.

Debrief time. For every question I'm going to name the correct answer, explain why each distractor was written the way it was, and call out the most common miss. If you got one wrong, listen for why — the wrong answer was designed to exploit a specific misunderstanding.

Q1. Correct answer is A, jamming. The question signals the attack plainly — high-power transmitter, frequency band flooding, preventing legitimate access. That is the textbook CB definition of jamming as a denial-of-service attack. The most common miss was C, war driving, because students see 'parking garage' and pattern-match to war driving. The deciding factor is always: is the attacker TRANSMITTING to disrupt, or RECEIVING to map? Transmitting to flood is jamming every time.

Q2. Correct answer is C. C has two separate errors: the anonymity claim is false per EK 1.3.C.1, and the end-to-end encryption claim is false — a VPN encrypts to the VPN operator only, not to the final destination. If you got this wrong, almost certainly it's because you missed the word INCORRECT. A, B, and D are all accurate CB statements — circling INCORRECT before you read choices would have locked this answer in immediately.

Q3. Correct answer is B, I and II only. III is the error — the analyst in the question got it wrong. Modifying an existing jamming tool to target a specific frequency band is exactly what CB calls high-skilled: 'modify existing ones to adapt.' That's not low-skilled because it required modifying the tool, not just deploying it as-is. If you picked D, you trusted the analyst. The question asked you to check the analyst's work.

Q4. Correct answer is D. A, B, and C are all genuine CB-required defenses — so if you picked one of those, you missed the word LEAST. D sounds reasonable as a security habit: change your password before using risky Wi-Fi. The problem is timing. The evil twin intercepts whatever you log in with during the session. If you just changed your password, the attacker gets the new one. Password rotation is a post-compromise response, not a pre-session defense. Circle LEAST before reading — this question rewards that habit.

Q5. Correct answer is C, war driving. Every detail in the scenario maps to EK 1.3.B.3 verbatim: detecting beacons, gathering network information, finding where the signal extends outside the building. The most common miss was D — students who know war driving is often legal assumed it couldn't be a CB-classified attack. Legality and CB classification are separate questions. EK 1.3.B.3 names war driving as a wireless attack type. That's the answer.

Before you leave: a 90-second bridge. Everything in 1.3 required an attacker to show up somewhere with equipment. That's a real constraint. Lesson 1.4 is about what happens when AI removes that constraint. The attacks scale. The personalization improves. A low-skilled adversary with an AI tool can generate spear-phishing content that previously required a skilled human to research and write. CB Scenario 1D is a deepfake impersonation scenario — same social engineering principles from 1.1, now running at machine speed and volume. Take a three-minute look at the 1.4 preview before you leave.

End of Lesson 1.3. You now have the three attacks, the three defenses, and the trade-offs between them. The HTTPS-doesn't-stop-credential-harvesting and VPN-shifts-trust points are both explicitly in the CED and both likely to appear on the AP exam. Lesson 1.4 picks up where this leaves off: same adversary classification framework, same social engineering principles, now with AI doing the heavy lifting.
